CLARITY.justification: The paper is broadly well-organized (Intro → dataset → method → experiments → conclusion) and the pipeline is described step-by-step (Sec. 3), but presentation problems make parts hard to follow. Notation is uneven and sometimes incomplete (e.g. "xl ∈ R ," missing dimensions in Sec. 3.1) and equations/footnotes are integrated awkwardly. Figures/tables are frequently referenced (Fig. 1, Fig. 3, Table 1/2/3) but not shown here and several paragraphs contain duplicated/garbled text and citation/grammar errors (repeated passages near the end), which reduces clarity and requires careful copyediting and reformatting.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper mainly assembles and engineers existing components rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms: e.g. the vision encoder (ResCeption) is inception-v3 augmented with residual shortcuts (Sec.3.1) — a design already explored in concurrent work — and the ROI detector is Faster R-CNN conditioned on a guided category (Sec.3.4), which is an application-level adaptation. Treating multi-label attribute prediction as sequence generation with an LSTM (Sec.3.2–3.3) is an incremental use of RNNs for label dependencies (the authors even cite similar work, Wang et al. (2016)), and the retrieval pipeline (attribute-based inverted index + binarized features) is an engineering combination of standard techniques. The main novelty is practical integration and a large internal dataset, but algorithmically the contributions are limited.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are stated and some empirical evidence is reported (e.g. ResCeption ILSVRC top-1/5 errors, CIDEr-D for COCO, dataset size and splits for attribute recognition), but the text omits key experimental details, baseline comparisons and numeric results referenced in tables/figures (e.g. Table 2/3, figures) making full validation difficult. The short mathematical derivation of residual shortcut gradients is straightforward and appears correct. The experimental methodology is weakly documented: no clear baselines or ablations for the RNN-as-sequence approach versus standard multi-label methods, limited ROI detection quantitative detail, and no statistical analysis. There are logical gaps/unsupported assumptions (e.g. simple threshold binarization justification, reliance on textual meta and guided-category benefits without complete quantitative evidence), so conclusions are only partially justified by the presented results.
